costs: Administrative redesign and validation costs for new admissions instruments
Ongoing negotiation costs with church/private maintainers who operate under admissions exemptions
Risk that redesign is only partially effective if socioeconomic advantage re-asserts itself through other channels (tutoring for new exam format)
content.equity_impact: Likely moderate positive: directly targets the mechanism (SES-independent sorting advantage, tutoring dependency) most proximate to admissions unfairness, but does not address the structural segregation drivers (geographic concentration, school choice, maintainer exemptions) identified as independent amplifiers.
expected_benefits: Reduces SES-driven access advantage without triggering the closure/job-loss costs seen in Poland's structural reversal
Directly responsive to Minister's own diagnosis that performance gains reflect family background rather than institutional value-added
Politically lower-salience than track abolition, potentially avoiding the 'existential threat' framing seen in current debate
content.goal: Keep the 6-/8-year track structure intact but replace performance/recommendation-based admissions with mechanisms less correlated with family background and private tutoring access.

implementation_steps.action: Launch the announced 'societal dialogue' on admissions-mechanism reform, including unions (PDSZ, PSZ) and Pedagogue Chamber
Redesign entrance-exam content and weighting to reduce tutoring-advantage sensitivity and reduce reliance on teacher recommendations
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Education and testing bodies

implementation_steps.action: Pilot revised admissions criteria in a subset of gimnáziums before national rollout
implementation_steps.actor: Klebelsberg Centre and school maintainers (state schools) plus negotiated church/private maintainer agreements

mechanisms: Redesigning entrance exams to reduce tutoring-sensitive content could weaken the SES-independent sorting advantage documented in Hungary versus other OECD countries
Administrative desegregation rules in the 2000s produced temporary reductions in urban segregation, indicating admissions-level intervention can shift outcomes even without structural abolition
Institution-by-institution accountability review based on competence-test results, as advocated by the National Pedagogue Chamber, could complement admissions redesign
risks: Church-maintained and private schools sit outside Klebelsberg Centre authority and may resist or opt out of admissions changes, limiting national coverage
Prior administrative desegregation measures 'stagnated mid-decade,' suggesting reform fatigue or reversal risk over time
Divided union positions (PSZ favoring phase-out vs PDSZ favoring broader segregation focus) may prevent unified implementation support
content.title: Preserve tracks, reform admissions mechanism (Lannert-style procedural reform)
